items.q: What is the gender of 'el día' (day)?
items.why: Students rely on '-a = feminine'. Common exceptions: el día, el mapa, el problema, el sistema, el clima (many Greek-origin -ma words). These appear constantly so must be memorised.

items.q: How do you say 'I like coffee' (in general) in Spanish?
items.why: English drops the article for general likes; Spanish keeps it. For general preferences, use the DEFINITE article: me gusta EL chocolate, me gustan LOS libros.

items.q: Why does 'Me gusta el chocolate' literally translate as 'Chocolate is pleasing to me'?
items.correct: Gustar works backwards - the thing liked is the subject, the person is the indirect object.
items.misconception: Gustar means 'to like' and works exactly like English.
items.random_wrong: It is a typo for 'Yo gusto'.
items.why: Students translate gustar as 'to like' with English structure. Actually gustar = 'to be pleasing to'. So 'me gustan los libros' (books are pleasing to me) - the verb agrees with the thing, not the liker.
Correct answer: Me duelen los pies. “Me duele los pies” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: I have a fever. “I'm tired” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many arms (brazos) does a person typically have?
items.why: The real figure is 2. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
